name: Tibetan Phurba
description: The Phurba, also known as a kīla or magic dagger, is a three-sided peg, stake, knife, or nail used in Tibetan Buddhism. Originating in pre-Buddhist shamanic traditions of the Himalayas, it was adopted and adapted into Vajrayana Buddhism as a ritual implement representing the forceful, wrathful activity of enlightened beings. The Phurba is primarily used for spiritual purposes, such as cutting through negative energies, binding demonic forces, and stabilizing the ground during tantric rituals. Its three blades symbolize the transmutation of the three poisons: ignorance, attachment, and aversion. The handle often features the faces of wrathful deities or protective spirits, further empowering its function. The practice of using a Phurba involves visualization, mantra recitation, and mudras (hand gestures), performed by qualified practitioners, often monks or tantric yogis. The Phurba is not typically used as a physical weapon but as a symbolic tool to pierce and subdue inner and outer obstacles to enlightenment. Its cultural significance lies in its embodiment of the power to overcome negativity and transform destructive forces into wisdom and compassion. The construction and consecration of a Phurba are also highly ritualized, often involving specific materials, colors, and blessings to enhance its efficacy.
subtitle: Cutting through negativity with ritual power.

keyMyths.title: Origin of Vajrakilaya
keyMyths.description: Vajrakilaya manifests to subdue the demonic forces hindering enlightenment. His Phurba represents the piercing of ignorance and the attainment of wisdom.

keyMyths.title: Subduing the Earth Goddess
keyMyths.description: The Phurba is used to stabilize the earth during tantric rituals. This action ensures the integrity and effectiveness of the ritual space.

sources.author: Khenpo Phuntsok Tashi
sources.description: Detailed explanation of Vajrakilaya practice and the use of the Phurba.
